context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:
Course context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. | Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|

Exercise:
TITLE: Concepts: Pro versus Anti-Development

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What do we make of anti-development arguments? Are they utopian or nihilist or neither? Can one be anti-development and still plan? What are the justifications for planning? Or is the division between pro and anti too Manichean? Why certain types of anti-planning arguments emerged at certain times? What was the historical context in which these criticisms emerged? What explains the rise, fall and now the latest rise of anti-development arguments?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m working on this 700–800 word reflection on pro vs anti-development, and I’m kinda lost on how to frame it. Could u help me figure out a main argument first?
exchanges.tutor: Good place to start! Before we think about framing, let me ask you: when you hear the term 'anti-development,' what do you think it actually means? What are these thinkers arguing against?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the start of the assignment and needs direction. Rather than scaffolding a framework for them, I should use the Socratic method to get them thinking about the core concepts first. I'll ask them a pointed question to get them to engage with the material and form their own initial view.

exchanges.student: I think it means they’re against big top-down development models, not literally against improving people’s lives? I’m not fully sure what exactly they reject tho.
exchanges.tutor: That's a useful instinct - you're already sensing a distinction between 'development' as an ideology or project versus the idea of improving lives. So here's a question: if anti-development thinkers aren't against improving lives, what specifically are they critiquing? Think about who defines 'development,' and whose interests that definition might serve.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a reasonable intuition - they're distinguishing between rejecting 'development' as a concept/project versus rejecting improvement in living conditions. This is a key distinction worth building on. I should affirm the direction of their thinking and push them to sharpen it with a follow-up question rather than elaborating for them.

exchanges.student: Maybe they’re critiquing how Western states or institutions define “progress” for other countries? Like tbh it can serve elites, donors, or colonial-type power more than local people.
exchanges.tutor: Good - you're touching on something real there about power and whose definition of progress counts. Now, the prompt asks a pointed question: does that critique make anti-development thinkers *utopian*, *nihilist*, or neither? What do you think those two labels would actually mean in this context?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good progress, connecting anti-development critique to power, Western dominance, and whose interests are served. This is solid and moves toward the core of post-development thinking. I should affirm this and now push them toward the prompt's harder questions - specifically whether this critique is utopian, nihilist, or neither, and whether one can still plan while holding this view.

exchanges.student: Ngl I’m a little stuck on those labels rn. Utopian would mean unrealistic ideal alternatives, and nihilist would mean just tearing down development without offering anything?
exchanges.tutor: Those definitions work as a starting point. Now apply them: based on what you said earlier about anti-development critiques, do you think they actually fall into either of those categories? Or somewhere else?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a reasonable working definition of both terms. Rather than confirming or expanding, I should push them to apply those definitions to actual anti-development arguments to see if they hold. This moves them toward their own analytical position for the paper.

exchanges.student: I think maybe neither, bc some critiques seem legit without being anti-everything. But idk how to say that in a sharper thesis.
exchanges.tutor: I won't help craft the thesis for you - that's yours to write. But here's what might help you get there: if anti-development is *neither* utopian nor nihilist, what would you point to as evidence? Think about whether these thinkers offer any alternatives, and what those alternatives look like.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is converging on a position but asking me to help sharpen their thesis - which would mean doing the writing for them, which I must refuse. Instead, I should redirect them to think about what evidence or reasoning would support their 'neither' position, which will naturally help them sharpen their own argument.

exchanges.student: Maybe the evidence is that they push participatory or local forms of planning instead of universal blueprints. So they’re rejecting one model of development, not all collective action I think.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong observation. Now connect it directly to the prompt's next question: if anti-development thinkers still support local, participatory forms of planning, what does that tell you about whether one can be *anti-development and still plan*?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a significant conceptual leap - distinguishing between rejecting a specific model of development versus rejecting collective action entirely. This directly sets up a strong answer to whether one can be anti-development and still plan. I should affirm this and push them toward that next question in the prompt.

exchanges.student: Yeah, then I’d say u can be anti-development and still plan, if planning means collective decision-making instead of technocratic control. Is that too broad?
exchanges.tutor: That's a meaningful distinction worth keeping. Now test it historically: the prompt asks why anti-development arguments emerged *at certain times*. When do you think these critiques gained traction, and what was happening in the world that might explain that timing?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is developing a solid conceptual argument and is now self-doubting. Rather than reassuring them or evaluating the quality of their claim directly, I should push them to test it themselves by thinking about the historical context question in the prompt, which will also add depth and nuance to their argument. We've covered several key conceptual points now so it's a good time to push toward the historical dimension.

exchanges.student: Maybe after postcolonial development projects disappointed ppl, like debt crises and failed modernization stuff in the 70s/80s? I’m guessing the critique grew when state-led and donor-led models looked kinda harmful or ineffective.
exchanges.tutor: That's well-reasoned. Now the prompt also asks about the *rise, fall, and latest rise* of anti-development arguments. If the critique rose in the 70s/80s, when do you think it fell, and what might explain its comeback today?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is correctly identifying a key historical moment - the crisis of modernization and state-led development in the 70s/80s. This is good and accurate. I should affirm and push them further to think about the 'rise, fall, and latest rise' arc mentioned in the prompt, which they haven't addressed yet and which will add important depth to their paper.

exchanges.student: I’m thinking it fell when globalization/neoliberalism got framed as the only realistic path, but now it’s back bc of inequality, climate crisis, and backlash to top-down aid models. Am I missing a big piece?
exchanges.tutor: You've built a solid foundation. One piece of the prompt you haven't tackled yet: is the pro vs. anti divide too *Manichean*? In other words, is the binary itself the problem? What do you think?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now covered most of the key conceptual terrain from the prompt: what anti-development means, utopian/nihilist labels, planning while being anti-development, and the historical arc. Their arc argument is solid. Rather than confirming or filling gaps, I should now push them to think about the final piece of the prompt they haven't fully addressed: the 'Manichean' question about whether the pro/anti divide is too simplistic. This is also a good moment to push toward integration since we've had several productive exchanges.

exchanges.student: Yeah imo that binary is too simple, bc a lot of people are prob pro-justice or pro-wellbeing but anti certain development ideologies. I think my paper should argue the real divide is over who defines progress and how planning gets legitimated.
exchanges.tutor: That argument connects well to the course's core themes around power and legitimacy. You now have enough to start writing. I'd encourage you to stop discussing and put your ideas into a draft - you've built the conceptual groundwork, now it needs to become your 700-800 words. What's your opening move?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now worked through all the major conceptual elements of the prompt and has arrived at a compelling central argument about legitimacy and who defines progress - which also connects well to the course's major theme about planning, power, and legitimacy. This is a good moment to push them to stop discussing and start writing, since we've had about 9 exchanges and they have enough material to draft their reflection.
